data.title: Nebius: At The Heart Of The AI Gold Rush
data.description:
Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) provides an advanced avenue for participation in the GPU infrastructure cycle. It brings to the mix two important things: compute for both training and inference, and cheap power solutions. The company builds where this is available and sells it under long‑term, take‑or‑pay type contracts with tier‑one customers. The five‑year contract with Microsoft provides even more long-term visibility regarding this side of the business.

My bullish case is based on three factors. Nebius builds where the economics of power and cooling are well defined, as in Finland and a behind‑the‑meter design in New Jersey; builds demand as in capacity it can deliver to stream but is without bare metal; and, laid immediately onto this, a cloud AI and inference service which will ramp up long‑term monetization opportunities. All of which spells potential multi‑billion dollar revenue, improving unit economics, and stable relationships with those interested in using AI.

A Different Kind of Cloud: The Rise of the Neoclouds

Hyperscalers still dominate compute, but demand is outstripping even their build cycles. A cadre of specialist suppliers has grown up, and this is usually referred to as the neoclouds. These are rich in GPUs, AI‑oriented and are happy with structured capacity contracts. Industry sources suggest that the total commitments from Microsoft to the neocloud suppliers run to many tens of billions of dollars, with Nebius being one of the largest. The contracts are set up to secure access to cutting‑edge components from Nvidia. Capacity has become as much of a supply chain issue as the furnishing of datacenter capacity.

Nebius is in this group. It has built clusters in New Jersey, Kansas City, Paris, Iceland and the UK, also concentrating on a flagship Nordic campus in Finland. The geographic split spreads power and permitting risk and brings capacity close to clients with regional latency requirements.

Implications of Microsoft Contract

The contract is likely to deliver contracted demand for the growth story. The Microsoft contract is the linchpin to revenues in the short‑term. Tranches are built in for 2025 and 2026. The contract runs to 2031. The obligation comes into play as soon as Nebius has put in place financing for each connected CAPEX, and inflows of cash will support the build. The protections include service level agreements, liquidated damages and termination rights for late deliveries after a cure period. Should Nebius execute on the plan, much of their near‑term revenues are already committed to blue‑chip customers. The relationship provides them with an on‑ramp to sell incremental services above that guaranteed capacity over time.

The Supply Chain in the Era of GB300

Access to the newest Nvidia parts is still a limitation on the industry. The recent description of Microsoft’s neocloud program indicates that the umbrella of agreements could secure it large volumes of the GB300‑class chips for its internal AI teams. As a named partner, Nebius will be well placed for Blackwell‑generation clusters and also the refresh thereafter. The participation of Nvidia in the 2024 private placement is yet another sign that Nebius fits in the ecosystem and will likely continue to remain on allocation as areas come online.

Nebius has a thoughtful balance toward its mix. The broadcast or pitch is simple. If a buyer has the ability to commit to scale and time, then Nebius will set aside capacity at attractive prices. This keeps utilization high whilst monetizing a long pipeline of demand.

The Platform Layer: More Than Bare Metal

Above the metal, Nebius operates an AI Cloud with managed Kubernetes. Above that is AI Studio, an inference‑at‑scale service with a simple API and a catalog of strong open models. Price is transparent through the million tokens for real‑time and batch processing. Where fixed contracts do not fully use the reserved GPUs, spare capacity can be directed into AI Studio. This creates operational leverage with a larger footprint.

There is also a branding aspect. Winning builders on the platform means there is an output demand channel beyond wholesale compute. A good mix with time will mean that contracted capacity for hyperscalers and services for enterprises that are on consumption‑based models will stabilize revenue/cash flows. This will also give Nebius the ability to present as differentiated from peers, being more cluster‑focused.

Competitors: Where Nebius Can Take Share

Straight comparisons are other neoclouds with Microsoft capacity deals to help with AI. Buyers will multisource. Nebius has three advantages: geography, power strategy and a product surface above the cluster. Finland’s efficiency and heat recovery are tough to replicate. A behind‑the‑meter solar model in New Jersey reduces a big risk that impacts pricing for a number of competitors. The AI Studio offering will attract developers and inference workloads that do not require long reservations.

In terms of hyperscalers, Nebius does not need to beat them. It has to be the best partner when the hyperscaler prefers to run AI compute as an operational expense. It also allows hyperscalers to offload customers that want sovereign, dedicated or region‑specific capacities. The Microsoft deal gives Nebius substantial credentials that procurement departments can point to when offering critical workloads to a specialist.

What will the next two years look like? The playbook is simple. Deliver the first tranches for Microsoft out of Vineland, keep adding power and cooling to that campus, and expand the Nordic and Midwest regions. Public disclosures indicate deployments for the Microsoft deal are scheduled in 2025 and 2026, and the contract runs to 2031. That time frame allows Nebius to pull forward multi‑year demand while finalizing other relationships. In contrast to the future, AI Studio, as it scales, should lead to adjustments in the mix toward a balance of wholesale capacity and consumption‑related services. If that occurs, Nebius could reach the end of 2026 with a higher revenue base, more efficient unit economics and a more compelling case to fund the next wave of builds from internal cash flow.

Risks

Execution risk is paramount. The Microsoft contract has liquidated damages and cancellation rights for missing a delivery. Obligations start only when Nebius confirms that it has achieved financing for related capital expenses. This results in a large incentive to sequence each tranche against already achieved capital and realistic construction schedules.

Supplies of energy are the other swing factor. This is why New Jersey has a behind‑the‑meter model and Finland relies on free cooling and heat reuse. The less that Nebius can be tied to congested grid supplies, and the more it can demonstrate that it can achieve superior energy‑use efficiency, the longer its cost advantage will last. The environmental side is real. The Mäntsälä heat recovery loop heats the equivalent of 2,500 homes a year.

There is also a historical overhang. Some investors will wonder about historic ties with Russia. The acts in 2024 and 2025 were designed to lessen this danger. The best antidote will be steady delivery on the Western contracts obtained and ongoing contact with U.S. and European capital markets.

Why I am Bullish

Nebius has three hard‑to‑copy features. It is part of the Microsoft neocloud program with a five‑year contract that can be modularly increased if more capacity is utilized. It develops where it can harvest structural advantages with energy costs, which should lead to a lowering of operating costs coupled with sustainably better outcomes. It is also developing a platform above the cluster, which gives an increasing probability that each region will feed not only wholesale buyers. This adds up to a potentially great scaling company in revenues, rising utilization, and finding itself in an ever‑more profitable margin structure in a filling footprint.

This is not a low‑risk stock. The build program looks ambitious. The financing program is large. Customer concentration is genuine. In a state of play where much of the AI infrastructure story remains aspirational, Nebius is delivering with an anchor signed agreement and visible delivery on the ground. This is precisely the setup I want to be part of.

Bottom line

Nebius is not aiming to be a general‑purpose cloud. It is designing for AI throughput per watt and per dollar and bringing that level of capability to the market through long‑term agreements and a growing platform surface. The Microsoft tie‑in gives run to the story. Finland and New Jersey present a cost advantage. AI Studio adds a lever to mix and monetization. Add in that if the team lead on the New Jersey tranche comes through, and grows out Finland and the Midwest, this could develop as a multi‑year compounder from an early revenue base. For those looking for a halfway house for direct play into the AI infrastructure cycle, away from the inside of a hyperscaler, Nebius deserves your attention.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Ferrari Revs Up Elettrica
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Ferrari’s first EV will have 1,000 horsepower and unique engine sound. (0:15) Silver tops $50. (2:06) Hollywood vs. AI. (2:50)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Ferrari (NYSE:RACE) unveiled some details about its first electric vehicle, which has a placeholder name of Elettrica.

The EV will have in-house-developed electric motors with 1,000 horsepower, going from 0 to 62 in 2.5 seconds. The Elettrica will have a range of over 323 miles and a top speed of 192 mph, powered by a 122-kWh battery pack.

But what about that signature Ferrai roar?

A system that uses real powertrain vibrations will create a novel, emotive electric Ferrari sound, settoing it apart from other EVs.

The initial skateboard platform indicates a four-door, but for now it’s unclear if it will be a sedan or SUV. The design debut is expected next spring.

But amid all this, Ferrari lowered its forecast for electric vehicles to account for 20% of its lineup by 2030, down from a prior target of 40%.

Among active stocks, PepsiCo (PEP) topped Q3 earnings expectations and named a new CFO. Walmart U.S. CFO Steve Schmitt will step into the role in a month.

Looking ahead, Pepsi expects a low-single-digit increase in full-year organic revenue and core constant currency EPS about even with the prior year.

Delta Air Lines (DAL) is rallying on solid results coupled with an upbeat outlook for the remainder of the year.

After a turbulent start to the year, strength in premium, corporate and loyalty boosted Q3 numbers above expectations. And it now sees the Q4 top and bottom lines beating current forecasts.

Nvidia (NVDA) nabbed a Street-high price target of $300 from Cantor analyst C.J. Muse, who reiterated his Top Pick Overweight, saying customers are “searching desperately for compute wherever they can find it.”

The next highest target is $275 from Melius Research, which boosted theirs from $240 earlier this week.

And cannabis stocks are gaining after Tilray Brands (TLRY) posted its first revenue beat in four quarters, boosted by its distribution business.

Canopy Growth (CGC), Aurora Cannabis (ACB) and SNDL (SNDL) and all following Tilray higher.

Looking to the markets, it’s High-Ho-Silver.

Echoing gold’s breakneck rally, spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) topped $50/oz. for the first time since 2011.

Research firm MenthorQ says the historic silver run is fueled by industrial demand, supply tightness and economic uncertainty are fueling this historic rally.

In other news of note, China tightened its rules on rare earth exports. Those elements are crucial to many high-tech products.

The announcement from the Ministry of Commerce clarifies and expands sweeping controls announced in April, which caused massive shortages around the world before a series of deals with Europe and the U.S. resumed shipments.

The new announcement makes it clear that licenses will likely be denied to arm makers and some semiconductor companies.

Also, technology used to mine and process the rare earths, or to make magnets from rare earths, can only be exported with permission from the government.

And we’ve seen this reel before.

Hollywood’s Creative Artists Agency said that OpenAI (OPENAI) is exposing artists to "significant risk" through its new video-generating tool Sora.

The talent agency told Reuters: "The question is, does OpenAI and its partner companies believe that humans, writers, artists, actors, directors, producers, musicians, and athletes deserve to be compensated and credited for the work they create?"

CAA said control, permission for use, and compensation were "a fundamental right" of creative workers and warned the misuse of new technologies poses risks beyond entertainment and media. But it added it was open to hearing OpenAI's solutions.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, it’s time to play the reel of short opportunities again.

Yesterday, we gave you J.P. Morgan’s best consumer stocks to short. Today it’s top tech shorts.

Two of the stocks are Mobileye Global (MBLY), whose expected revenue growth does not support its valuation, especially given current challenges in the auto sector.

And Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), which keeps a Neutral rating on iPhone opportunities, but will be an underperformer compared to the broader semiconductor group.

Check out the other two names in our story.

data.title: Copper's Long-Term Bullish Trend Supports Higher Mining Stocks
data.description:
Copper is not the most liquid base metal trading on the London Metals Exchange, nor is it the most expensive. However, copper has long been the leader of other nonferrous metals, establishing the price path that the other metals follow. Copper, aluminum, nickel, lead, zinc, and tin trade on the world’s most liquid and flexible exchange, the LME in the United Kingdom. Copper futures trade on the CME’s COMEX division in the United States. The LME and COMEX authorize a network of approved warehouses that store the red nonferrous metal.

Meanwhile, the price differential, or arbitrage, between LME copper forwards for three-month delivery and COMEX copper futures can provide clues about the metal’s price path. Over the past few months, U.S. government policies have caused a massive distortion in the historical price relationship between the copper forwards and futures, leading to wild price swings. However, the global copper market remains in a deficit, where the supplies are struggling to keep pace with demand. The situation is likely to persist into the 2030s. Buying copper on price weakness has been optimal since the turn of this century, and fundamentals support a continuation of the bullish trend.

While the path higher is bumpy, copper mining stocks are likely to continue to benefit from copper’s ascent.

Tariffs caused volatility

U.S. tariffs caused extreme price variance in many commodities in 2025, and copper was no exception. The price action in the COMEX futures and LME forwards highlights the impact of trade barriers on the leading nonferrous metal.

The monthly chart shows that the COMEX futures rose to a record $5.1990 per pound in May 2024. Tariffs caused U.S. supply concerns, sending the COMEX futures to a higher $5.3740 per pound peak in March 2025. After the first wave of tariffs excluded copper, the price fell to a low of $4.03 in April before exploding to a higher peak of $5.9585 in July 2025. The futures were highly volatile, but the LME forwards remained below the May 2024 high in 2025 as the price differential, or arbitrage, widened to unprecedented levels because of the uncertainty created by U.S. tariffs.

The LME three-month copper forward chart illustrates that while the futures rose to record highs in March and July 2025, the LME price remained below the 2024 peak. The highs in COMEX copper futures reflected the trade barriers.

Copper moved from LME to COMEX warehouses

The U.S. trade policy led to copper and other commodities being relocated from European and other warehouses to the United States.

According to the LME website, at the end of 2024, LME copper inventories stood at 271,400 metric tons. Copper in COMEX warehouses was 93,161 metric tons. At the end of September 2025, the LME stock had declined 47.16% to 143,400 tons, while COMEX copper stocks rose 248% to 324,087 tons. The bottom line is that at the end of last year, the LME held nearly three times as much copper as COMEX. At the end of Q3 2025, U.S. tariffs caused a migration of copper from LME to COMEX warehouses, resulting in over 2.25 times more copper in COMEX than LME inventories. The stockpile flows caused substantial price volatility, increasing the demand for copper in the United States in anticipation and response to tariffs and less demand in Europe and other LME warehouse locations.

LME forwards are validating the current rally

The July announcement of a 50% tariff on some copper moving to the United States caused the COMEX futures price to plunge 27.3% from $5.9585 on July 24 to a low of $4.3325 on July 31.

The daily four-month chart shows the 21.4% recovery rally from the July 31 low of $4.3325 to the Oct. 9 high of $5.2610 per pound.

Meanwhile, LME three-month copper forwards moved 13% higher from $9,571.50 per ton on July 31 to the most recent high of $10,815 per ton on Oct. 8. More significantly, LME forwards are in a bullish trend at the highest price in 2025, with the upside technical target at the May 2024 record high of $11,104.50 per metric ton.

The bottom line is that the recent price action in the LME forwards validates the copper futures market rally, meaning higher highs could be on the horizon.

Production issues and a Citi upgrade cause volatility in a leading copper producer’s share price

Shares of copper-producing companies have been volatile. A recent production disruption at Indonesia’s Grasberg Mine caused a substantial correction in Freeport-McMoRan (FCX).

The chart highlights FCX’s over 24% decline from $46.29 on Sept. 23 to the low of $35.15 on Sept. 25. On Oct. 7, Citi upgraded FCX shares despite the Grasberg situation, based on its projection for $12,000 copper during the first half of 2026. FCX shares were trading around the $43.50 level on Oct. 9.

Copper remains in a bullish trend supported by technical and fundamental factors- ICOP is a diversified copper and metal mining ETF product

From a fundamental perspective, copper demand is soaring. Copper has expanded its addressable market from an industrial building block to an ingredient in many green energy initiatives. As mine supplies struggle to keep pace with the growing demand, the International Copper Study Group’s latest forecast expects a swing from years of modest surpluses to a 150,000-ton deficit in refined copper in 2026. China dominates copper demand, and an improvement in the Chinese economy may only cause a higher deficit over the coming years.

Technically, copper’s bullish trend remains firmly intact.

The chart shows copper’s bullish pattern of higher lows and higher highs since the 2001 low of 60.40 cents per pound. Moreover, even severe corrections have held previous lows, in nearly every case, over the past nearly two and one-half decades. Higher lows that hold above technical support levels are a clear sign that demand remains robust and buyers emerge during price corrections.

While producers will struggle to keep pace with the explosive copper demand, the leading copper mining companies will benefit from higher prices. I favor a diversified investment approach. The top holdings of the iShares Copper and Mining ETF (NASDAQ:ICOP) include:

ICOP has exposure to many of the world’s leading copper and metals mining companies. At $38.98 per share, ICOP had $96.73 million in assets under management. ICOP trades an average of approximately 37,000 shares daily and charges a 0.47% management fee. The $0.72 dividend translates to a 1.85% yield, covering the expense ratio in around one quarter.

The chart shows that since the April 7 tariff-inspired low, the ICOP ETF has rallied over 88.6%, moving from $21.10 to $39.80 per share on Oct. 9, 2025. If copper continues to make higher highs, expect ICOP to follow suit, as earnings for the leading copper miners are likely to increase.

While Seeking Alpha awards the highest possible ETF Grade for ICOP’s momentum, the 0.47% management fee receives a C+. While the assets and daily trading volume deserve a C- and the wild price swings in copper justify a D in risk, the 1.85% yield likely deserves a higher than D- dividend grade.

I rate copper and the ICOP ETF as "Buys," as LME copper is now justifying the latest rally in the COMEX futures. When fundamental and technical factors agree, the odds of a continuation of the current bullish price action are high.

data.title: PepsiCo: Flat Q3 Results, But Rising Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) has been left behind, if not actually punished by the market, as investors work on understanding AI supply chains, identifying the key players the hidden bottlenecks, or simply betting big on the hyperscalers. If we consider the last three months, Pepsi has lost 4%, while the market has gained 7%. Pepsi's dividend has surely helped in closing the gap a bit, but we are still talking about an underperformance of roughly 10 percentage points in a quarter. At the same time, several investors, particularly those who are income-oriented or seek defensive plays, are eyeing the stock, as its +4% dividend doesn't go unnoticed.

In the meantime, we learned that Elliott Investment Management stepped in and invested $4B in the company to take a stake and play a role in pushing for a turnaround. In the letter that was sent to Pepsi's management, Elliott pointed out that it wants Pepsi to resume growth, pointing out that its beverages business has long underperformed, even though the snacking business usually offsets the decline. One of the big changes that Elliott asked Pepsi to make was to spin the bottling business off. This could help Pepsi close the margin gap with Coca-Cola, which is now almost embarrassing. We see that Coca-Cola has a gross profit margin that is 6% above Pepsi's. But as we move down toward the bottom line, we see that the gap widens to a staggering 17%, with Pepsi's net income margin being only 8.23%.

Elliott sees an opportunity, considering that PepsiCo is deeply undervalued compared to its average valuation and the Consumer Staples Index.

Moreover, the activist fund pointed out that Pepsi has been a disaster for the past 10 years, with a cumulative underperformance over any period during the past two decades.

Insider selling and buying statistics show that many independent directors are buying the stock heavily, suggesting that they are bullish on Pepsi's prospects. Let's then see PepsiCo's results since the stock is trading up almost 3% after they were released.

PepsiCo's Q3 Earnings Review

The earnings report shows that Pepsi is growing its sales, but that its profits moved south. Pepsi was able to beat estimates, with net sales $90M above what analysts expected and coming above the consensus EPS by $0.03.

Net sales for the quarter moved up to $23.94B (Q3 2024: $23.32B), but the company's gross profit compressed to $12.82B (Q3 2024: $12.92), showing that the first hurdle was higher input costs. SG&A was also up by almost $100M, and this created further pressure on the operating profit, which was down to $3.57B (Q3 2024: $3.87B). The bottom line shows a $327M decline to $2.6B, causing the company's EPS to be $1.90 (Q3 2024: $2.13). So, the stock is up because the report was not as bad as expected. Nonetheless, PepsiCo is still facing several issues, both in its cost structure and in market share losses. The proof is the company's guidance, which expects this fiscal year to report an LSD revenue increase, with EPS that will probably be lower vs. FY2024. We also see that analysts were expecting organic volume to be down only by 1.33%, but it was reported that it was down 3%. So the main driver of PepsiCo's revenue growth was 4% pricing. When prices go up and volumes go down, the explanation is one and only one: consumers signal some kind of resistance to PepsiCo's new price hikes. As a result, I think PepsiCo will have to rely less on pricing and accept further margin compression to hold (and perhaps try to grow) its market share.

If we look at the regional mix, we see that North America Foods was barely flat (-0.2% YoY), while the beverage segment was finally able to report positive growth of 2.1%. It was the EMEA region that helped PepsiCo's results with sales growth of 5.5% YoY. However, North America Beverages and LatAm Foods were the two segments that tumbled and caused the operating profit of the company to be down 11%.

Were these results surprising? Not really. We have been seeing for several months that consumer disposable income matters and that many food companies talk about trade-down behaviors. Moreover, packaged foods cost more because of import tariffs on aluminum. Finally, we should not overlook PepsiCo's biggest threat: consumers are shifting their preferences toward protein, avoiding sugar. This is why PepsiCo spent $2B in March to acquire Poppi and gain exposure to prebiotic soda.

Valuation

The biggest catalyst for Pepsi is the potential turnaround. If Pepsi sells its bottling business and manages to raise margins, the stock will react positively. Right now, the stock trades at a 2025 P/E of 17, and if the company manages to barely grow its EPS next year, we could be talking about a 2026 P/E of around 16. Coca-Cola trades around a 22x-23x. This means that the opportunity is clear. If investors are convinced that Pepsi will improve its operations, becoming a leaner company, then a rerating could push the stock up by 20-25%. For example, if Pepsi does report 2026 EPS of $8.50 and shows in the meantime that it is becoming more profitable, it could be valued with a 20x multiple, which would cause the stock to jump to $170 (+20% from today's price). It would probably be a market-beating stock in 2026. If we consider the stock only for its organic business, the environment we are seeing doesn't make me think PepsiCo's sales will gain traction anytime soon.

As Bloomberg shows, over two-thirds of Elliott's targets reported an average gain of 35% one year after it was disclosed that the activist fund had stepped into the shareholder structure. These returns should not be expected, and they have to be taken with caution. After all, they come from an average of many unrelated investments that have only one point in common: Elliott.

There is also another reason to hold Pepsi right now, apart from its 4% yield. It could play a defensive role in the portfolios of bearish investors who are expecting a downturn. In this case, Pepsi would probably enjoy favorable momentum.

All in all, I am upgrading Pepsi to a buy. I am not convinced by its fundamentals and by the macroeconomic data. However, I think that when Elliott moves several billion, the likelihood of a positive outcome over a 12-24 month period increases. In this case, PepsiCo will probably be part of my portfolio as a satellite that can play, only in this situation, a double role: market-beating play and stabilizer.

data.title: Nissan's 2026 Leaf Battery Electric Arrives Amid Uncertain Future For Automaker
data.description:
Was it just 15 years ago that Japan’s Nissan Motor Corp. (OTCPK:NSANY) mounted a bold initiative to lead the global auto industry to a world of affordable, mass-market zero-emission vehicles with the debut of its Leaf battery electric?

Much has changed since then, notably battery electric (BEV) technology as well as the politics that initially nurtured global zero-emission policies and mandates. Nissan, meanwhile, is fighting for survival in a world shaped by fierce competition from Chinese BEVs in their home market and in Europe, as well as slower than expected demand for BEVs in the U.S. and cancellation of U.S. government incentives.

Internal management upheavals, notably the 2018 ouster of CEO Carlos Ghosn, mastermind and champion of Nissan’s BEV ambitions, have compounded the automaker’s competitive troubles. Renault S.A., the French automaker and onetime rescuer that holds a large stake in Nissan, isn’t in much better shape.

Best version

The 2026 Leaf crossover, now in its third generation, is vastly improved in terms of appearance, size, and performance over the original. Yet it arrives to a global marketplace where hundreds of BEV manufacturers vie for the attention of motorists – some who are notably enthusiastic for the new technology and others who remain skeptical at the limitations of battery range and charging infrastructure.

Car and Driver magazine calls Nissan’s new Leaf “a clean sheet reset” of the original with 300 miles of range (compared to 73 miles back in 2010) and a starting price at around $30,000. Manufactured in Japan and thus subject to U.S. tariffs, the model isn’t likely to have much positive impact on Nissan’s financials. In fact, Nissan said in late September it's discontinuing import to the U.S. of its larger and more expensive Ariya BEV, which starts at about $40,000, so it can deploy more resources toward Leaf.

In its latest fiscal year, ended March 30, Nissan posted a loss of $4.47 billion on total revenue of $84.2 billion. In fiscal 2018, the year Ghosn left, Nissan posted a net profit of about $7 billion on revenue of $112.5 billion.

Weather affected

The battery that powered the first Leaf was air-cooled, which resulted in range degradation due to extremes of weather; the latest version is liquid-cooled, one reason why the expected range is substantially greater.

The original Leaf, with ambitions to lead the EV market, was designed with a charging port based on the CHAdeMO format developed for Japan at a time when public charging ports were sparse in the U.S. Tesla (TSLA), with its NACS port standard, proved more popular because Tesla installed its own charging infrastructure and its models sold in far greater numbers than the Leaf to U.S. buyers. Significantly, the new Leaf now features an NACS port that can be used at Tesla chargers as well as a second port – CCS format – that can be used with a wide range of home and public chargers.

As Toyota Motor (TM) has demonstrated with strong sales and market share increases, motorists are more likely to migrate to vehicle models powered by gas-electric hybrid systems from internal combustion (ICE) than to BEVs. Toyota’s hybrid systems plus BEVs soon will account for half of unit sales in the profitable U.S. market.

Nissan currently offers no gas-electric hybrid models in the U.S. However, it plans soon to introduce a plug-in hybrid (PHEV) Rogue Crossover, followed by an extended-range hybrid system that features a gasoline engine that charges a battery that powers one or more electric motors. According to reports, Nissan is discussing the possible sharing of the extended-range system with Ford Motor Co. (F) and Stellantis N.V. (STLA).

The 2026 Leaf begins arriving at U.S. dealerships and will go on sale in Japan this month. Having driven the new model, I can report that it's a pleasant, well-handling vehicle that's quiet – as a BEV should be – and delivers reasonable pickup. The infotainment system is cleverly designed, and the overall exterior appearance is quite pleasing. Leaf should serve as a second car for families that take long trips or as a primary vehicle for customers with garages equipped with a Level 2 (220 amp) charger.

Cloudy forecast

Yet the overall prospects for Leaf and other BEVs are muted in the U.S., at least in the short term.

As reported in the Financial Times:

AlixPartners has nearly halved its EV forecast for the US and now expects pure EVs to account for 7 per cent of car sales in 2026, with petrol making up 68 per cent and hybrids 22 per cent. Even in 2030, EVs are now expected to make up only 18 per cent in the US — sharply lower than the 40 per cent in Europe and 51 per cent in China.

In May, Nissan CEO Ivan Espinosa unveiled a recovery plan set to unfold over the next two years that “must prioritize self-improvement with greater urgency and speed, aiming for profitability that relies less on volume” and reduces fixed and variable costs by 500 billion yen, or about $3.26 billion, compared with current levels. Eventually, 20,000 jobs will be cut, along with seven of 17 plants.

With a Hold Quant rating from Seeking Alpha and outside analysts, Nissan shares look speculative at their current price, which is about a quarter of the price when Ghosn was forced out seven years ago. The risk of holding NSANY shares today has actually grown since 2018 as the Chinese auto industry makes deeper inroads in Europe, Latin America, and Southeast Asia – although not yet in the U.S.

An initiative to merge Nissan and Honda Motor Co. (HMC) failed earlier this year following discussions between the companies. The Japanese government could step in, as it sometimes does, to force a merger or accelerated restructuring if it sees that Nissan’s current turnaround effort is falling short.

From an investor’s perspective, Nissan is a stock to avoid until and unless its current turnaround bears fruit in terms of reignited growth of sales and profit, as well as a demonstrated ability to compete with top brands from around the world, especially China.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell's Financial Playbook: AI Infrastructure, Margin Expansion, Shareholder Returns
data.description:
Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) raised the bar for margin-accretive growth for the coming years as AI infrastructure investments far outweigh previous forecasts. As enterprise AI adoption remains in its infancy, Dell should realize significant tailwinds as enterprises and cloud services providers [CSPs] upgrade aging infrastructure to support the capacity and power demand of AI applications. With the improved outlook, I am reiterating my Buy rating for Dell shares with a price target of $191/share at 9.92x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Dell Technologies Operations

Dell came to its 2025 Analyst Day with the utmost optimism as the capital spend for AI infrastructure grows far beyond any previous expectations, increasing from a previous forecast of $200b for 2025 to $400b. This is largely supported by the major cloud services providers [CSPs], who almost unanimously increased guidance for their respective capital outlays for the remainder of 2025 and for 2026. Based on my analysis of Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG), Oracle (ORCL), and Amazon (AMZN), I’m forecasting the capital outlay to sum to $360 billion [net of Oracle] to support the aggregate cloud service backlog of $1.24 trillion [net of Meta Platforms]. Based on the growing backlogs among the hyperscalers, it is clear that significant investments in building out compute capacity will be necessary for the coming years, particularly when considering these cloud services are high-margin revenue sources.

Dell primarily services the Tier 2 CSPs, sovereigns, and enterprises, an area of the market that is expected to grow significantly in the coming years. This in part will be driven by a major server refresh cycle for servers that are 14th generation or older, which account for 70% of Dell’s installed base. This could potentially be driven by enterprise AI adoption and the need for more powerful IT infrastructure to support compute demand. Accordingly, Dell has 6,700 unique customers that can drive significant growth in the transition to AI infrastructure. Though the refresh cycle has been lagging in recent quarters, the transition to enterprise AI could be the kick-start Dell needed in order to deliver growth.

Another driver worth considering is the reduced data center footprint with 17th generation Dell servers; with improved power efficiency on a smaller scale, particularly as power costs increase, enterprises may be compelled to invest in newer, cost-effective technologies.

Specific to hardware spend, Dell’s leadership expects capital spend to grow to $310b by 2027, a major step up from previous estimates. Driving the matter is enterprise GenAI adoption being in the early stages of broad adoption. Accordingly, management indicated that only 1% of enterprises have a mature deployment of GenAI, with 87% of enterprises expecting to more heavily utilize GenAI for business operations.

Dell is taking a page out of its own book by applying the foundation for AI implementation across the organization to improve its cost structure. Dell has implemented coding assistants and knowledge assistants in its R&D department to improve cycle time for product development. Dell has also implemented customer service agents for its service desk to improve efficiency and productivity, a sales chat assistant to improve its sales department, and predictive systems to enhance the supply chain.

In order to improve the devices business, leadership laid out three key drivers: go-to-market strategy; more marketable devices; and expanded peripherals. The go-to-market strategy is to target emerging enterprises with Dell Pro Essential, offering enterprise devices at an entry price point. In terms of expanding its device offerings and peripherals, management is aiming to capture more of the PC market share as well as accessories like Dell-branded keyboards, speakers, cameras, etc., with the goal of expanding its total addressable market and improving profitability.

Dell Technologies Financial Position

Management laid out a strong outlook for long-term growth through 2030, increasing growth expectations across both ISG and CSG. Long-term growth for CSG was increased to 7-9%, up from 2-3%, and ISG is now targeting 11-14%, up from 6-8%, through 2030. In aggregate, total revenue to Dell is now expected to grow at a rate of 7-9%. In addition to this, management is expecting increased scale and operational efficiencies to drive margins, now targeting 15% growth for the adjusted EPS, up from the previous guidance of 8%.

In optimizing its working capital outlay, management is anticipating a strong cash flow conversion rate of 100%. Accordingly, management is aiming to return 80%+ of free cash flow to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends.

Looking ahead to Q3 2026, I’m forecasting Dell to generate $26.95b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.51/share. Growth will largely be driven by the server refresh cycle, AI infrastructure sales, and improved sales for consumer and commercial PCs. Given the strong growth trajectory for AI infrastructure, I’m forecasting ISG margins to experience a modest decline from the previous year’s quarter.

Dell closed Q2 2026 in a position of financial strength with $8b in cash & equivalents and $28.7b in debt on the balance sheet for a leverage ratio of 1.76x net debt/aEBITDA. I’m expecting Dell’s leverage ratio to improve in the coming quarter as management aims to reduce exposure to 1.50x.

Risks Related to Dell Technologies

Bull Case

The outlay for AI infrastructure spend continues to increase as customer interest outweighs supporting compute capacity, driving significant demand for hardware deployment. Though AI infrastructure is margin-destructive, Dell should realize margin support resulting from enterprise customers refreshing aging servers as they adopt GenAI and agentic AI. Dell is focusing on improving operational performance by deploying AI across the enterprise, potentially driving incremental margin improvements in the coming years.

Bear Case

The server refresh cycle is taking longer than anticipated, potentially being pushed out as a result of the challenging macroeconomic environment. With employment figures coming into question, enterprises may continue to push out new infrastructure purchases until there is more certainty of AI’s functionality and cost-saving abilities.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Management remains committed to enhancing shareholder value by returning 80%+ of free cash flow through dividend growth and share repurchases. The diluted share count was reduced by -3% in Q2 2026.

DELL shares currently trade at 11.41x EV/aEBITDA, a premium to its midpoint of roughly 10.71x. Given the recent run-up in the share price, I believe shares may be pricing in much of the growth through the next year; however, looking out longer term, I anticipate shares have some room for growth under the presumption that management can execute on their margin-accretive growth strategy.

Valuing DELL shares, I believe shares should be priced at $191/share at 9.92x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am reiterating my Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: U.S. Stocks Sector Divergence Raises Red Flags
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Equities have enjoyed a remarkable run, with major indices pushing to fresh highs - from last Friday’s Dow peak above the 47,000 milestone to yesterday’s record closes in the S&P 500 and Nasdaq.

Since June-July 2025, following the end of the 12-day Israel-Iran conflict, markets have been ecstatic, printing new all-time highs almost every week.

Yet, beneath the surface, some divergences are starting to show.

The S&P 500 now stands 9.5% above its January 2025 high, the Nasdaq is up 12.5%, but the Dow Jones has gained only 3.6% - a notable gap that hints at sectoral imbalance.

A weekly look at US Indices

This underperformance of consumer defensives and cyclicals, coupled with persistent USD strength, weighs on broader sentiment.

Tariffs are hurting US manufacturing companies and dragging profit margin expectations lower.

Things don't look as bad when a sector outperformance drags sentiment higher and pulls indices upward.

On the other hand, a lack of market breadth ends up dragging the stability of the overall sentiment lower. This is what is dragging indices lower in today's session.

Market breadth helps to gauge the overall health, direction, and participation within a stock market or index. It achieves this by comparing the number of stocks that are advancing against those that are declining.

How large are the sector divergences?

Top-performing sectors this year:

  • Basic Materials (dragged higher by commodities): +28.90%
  • Communication Services: +24.18%
  • Technology: +23.09%

Worst-performing sectors this year:

  • Consumer Defensive: +1.52%
  • Real Estate: +3.71%
  • Consumer Cyclical: +4.41%

At some point, market participants may take caution from some key sectors to the economy underperforming, leading to an overall reduced activity and purchasing power.

Now, a trader's role is to spot if this leads to a simple retracement that prompts dip buying, or if the current highs are established for a longer run.

For this, the best is to look at the current highs on all indices: If buyers can push for a weekly close above previous highs, it usually means that the trend is to continue.

If they fail to do so, markets will be looking to retest lower levels in value consolidation.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UTF: Finally, It's Time To Double Down While The Rest Panic
data.description:
Infrastructure investors were treated to a realization that, sometimes, it might be better to ply their trade on peers listed in The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLU), as the fund broke into a new high over the past week, while the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) CEF continues to underperform markedly. The underperformance has continued a downtrend bias for the past few years. Similar observations were also noted when considering the significant underperformance against Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), an AI infrastructure-focused CEF play that has also taken the market by storm, outperforming XLU and UTF significantly in the past year. It shows that leverage, when applied correctly, has the potential to bolster returns. In contrast, UTF's underperformance is also a stark reminder for investors that stock selection coupled with leverage could dramatically alter the near-term trajectory, even though companies represented in the utilities sector comprise the majority (in UTG's and UTF's case) or all of their exposure (in XLU's case).

Nevertheless, I think it's also apt to consider that UTF fund managers saw a recent opportunity to announce a "transferable rights offering," with the objective of securing even more ammo to partake more aggressively in current infrastructure buildout opportunities. Still, it didn't sit too well with the market, as investors hit back at UTF hard last month, leading to a sharp pullback in September that the fund has yet to fully recover from. While the buying support appears to be taking shape, I think the trepidation of not having clarity yet until the expiration of the offer in mid-October will likely continue to weigh down more robust buying sentiment in the near term.

In my previous UTF update, I indicated the fund's exposure to a diversified basket of opportunistic infra plays across several optionalities. These include optionalities including exposure to several themes such as utilities, industrials, and energy. They also consider prospects relating to reshoring, and of course, the AI data center plays. And how could we simply ignore the setup for the AI infra buildout potentially reaching into the multi-trillion dollar mark through the rest of the decade if our AI overlords get what they wish for. While I'm not doubting that there's a wide range of outcomes that could throttle the bullish AI themes if monetization and utilization couldn't keep up, the infra players to which UTF is exposed are well primed to extract substantive value from these openings, in my humble opinion. Therefore, the leverage factor could still play in favor of UTF in its subsequent recovery, aligned with what seems like a more dovish Fed, in which the market is betting on more rate cuts in the year ahead.

UTF: The market seems too skeptical, no?

Yet, let's not ignore the fact that the market appears to have taken their skepticism too far, notwithstanding the apprehensions that I highlighted earlier. While arguably justified, I think we also shouldn't downplay the positively skewed risk/reward on UTF right now, with the 6.5% discount to NAV at the widest in the past year. I'm not trying to downplay such risks, as the market has justified reasons to feel cautious about a re-rating right now. Yet, UTF's earnings multiple of 17.7x is not just markedly lower than the S&P 500's (SPX) (SPY) nearly 23x forward multiple, but also clearly below UTG's 21x multiple. Don't you think that the market could have taken their skepticism about UTF's near-term headwinds way too far? After all, if we consider UTF's 10Y total return of 174%, it isn't that far off relative to XLU's 180% or UTG's 186%. I'm aligned with the bullish camp in thinking that such a disconnect could be closed subsequently as the market reallocates toward a higher yield UTF at 7.6%, as compared to UTG's 5.9% or XLU's 2.54%.

UTF: Uptrend Anchors Remain Robust

I think what's also critical to note is that the recent plunge hasn't altered the uptrend bias observed in UTF since its bear market nadir back in late 2023. Moreover, UTF's recent volatility isn't new and certainly not the most damaging over the past two to three years. And what's crucial is that buying sentiment appears to have improved over the past two weeks, indicating dip-buyers have returned to the fold to help stabilize the stock, taking advantage of the panic selling that has also led to UTF dropping quickly into the oversold zone. And with UTF's distribution yield printing 7.6%, I believe it offers income investors a relatively attractive proposition while considering the offensive (leverage) and defensive (distributions) characteristics of the CEF.

And we must also consider that while the recent pullback could be attributed to the ambivalence and dilution uncertainties linked to the rights issuance, investors could have downplayed the secular opportunities that have also been strengthened as interest rates have clearly peaked, with the Fed having resumed the rate reduction regime this month.

Therefore, less onerous cyclical cost drivers (including lower interest expense linked to its leverage, currently at 2.47%) should help improve the overall return profile for holders. And for income investors seeking more attractive income investing opportunities with a secular growth blueprint, UTF is expected to become even more pertinent, given the recent plunge, offering a clear disconnect relative to its utilities peers. Hence, I think if you didn't manage to add exposure to UTF's bullish thesis previously, the current bifurcation could offer you another opportunity to reconsider hopping onto the infra bandwagon.

Nevertheless, it's always critical to contemplate the bearish side of any bullish narrative, notwithstanding the potential bottoming observed in UTF. Uncertainties relating to the rights issuance could weigh down a near-term re-rating until the expiration of the offer in mid-October. In addition, medium- to long-term infrastructure growth could be constrained by the long lead time needed to bolster electricity supply to meet a wide variety of underlying needs, on top of the insatiable AI capex buildout. Also, material risks relating to infra overbuild isn't immaterial as the medium-term revenue funding gap of $800B could compel a painful infra under-utilization and digestion phase that could also throttle the valuations of utilities-linked funds or stocks that are primarily exposed to the optimism embedded in the AI infra value chain.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Zeta Global: Tracking Well Against Long-Term Plan, Trading At Great Valuation
data.description:
As we look ahead to preparing our portfolios for 2026, caution should be at the top of every investor's mind. In particular, we should be cognizant of the fact that large-cap tech stocks have dominated most of the market's gains in 2025 and that we should be rotating into more value-oriented names to protect ourselves from downside.

Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (NYSE:ZETA), in my view, is a great stock that checks off many boxes. The company is executing well against its long-term FY28 targets while still getting support from potential inbound M&A interest. Despite a multitude of tailwinds, shares of Zeta are up only ~7% this year, giving investors a great entry point.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Zeta in May, when the stock was trading just under $14 per share. Since then, Zeta has rallied ~50%, with the company showcasing accelerating growth rates and strong KPIs ahead of the company's plans to roughly double adjusted EBITDA and revenue by FY28. I remain at a "Buy" rating here.

To illustrate the incredible momentum that Zeta has on its side, let's first discuss Zeta's three-year plan. By FY28, the company has a target of hitting $2.1 billion in revenue (implying a ~20% growth CAGR) and improving its adjusted EBITDA margins from a current ~20% to 25%, also implying adjusted EBITDA growing by nearly 2x.

The chart below, meanwhile, tracks the company's progress against the various metrics that drive its financial progress. We're particularly impressed by the company's growth in scaled customers (those who generate greater than >$100k in ARR). In its most recent quarter, Zeta hit a total count of 567 scaled customers, up 21% y/y and well ahead of the 4%-8% target in its three-year plan.

We note as well that Zeta's growth in quota-carrying sales reps is well ahead of its 2028 plan, even though opex is still growing modestly and allowing adjusted EBITDA expansion well ahead of plan. We think this is a strong signal to potential M&A acquirers.

That said, I don't think investors need to rely on a potential acquisition scenario to continue seeing gains on the stock, especially as Zeta's current valuation leaves plenty of room for upward multiples re-rating alongside strong performance. At current share prices near $20, Zeta trades at a market cap of $4.78 billion. After we net off the $365.3 million of cash and $196.7 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Zeta's resulting enterprise value is $4.61 billion.

Against the company's midpoint guidance ranges for FY25, Zeta trades at:

  • 3.7x EV/FY25 revenue.
  • 17.4x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

When we stack up Zeta against a number of other software companies that are growing at a high teens/low 20s percent, Zeta trades at a substantial discount:

In other words, I believe investors have multiple paths to appreciation in Zeta shares. Continued beat-and-raise quarters and evidence of progression toward Zeta's FY28 plans should continue to bring its multiples closer in line with peers, and at the same time, the hanging prospect of an acquisition may also deliver tremendous upside.

Stay long here and keep riding the upside in this stock.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Zeta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Zeta's revenue grew 35% y/y to $308.4 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $296.6 million (+30% y/y) by a five-point margin. We note, as shown in the chart below, that Zeta's organic growth, excluding its acquisition of LiveIntent as well as the tough comp from political-related spending in Q2 of last year was 27% y/y. This growth rate, meanwhile, accelerated slightly versus Q1.

As previously noted, Zeta has excelled at landing large new customers. The company added nine net-new customers, generating over $1 million in ARR, and total scaled customers of 567 grew 21% y/y, well ahead of the 4%-8% required for the company's three-year plan through FY28.

Management notes that the company has a full pipeline for the second half of FY25, giving Zeta healthy revenue visibility and strong confidence in its outlook for the remainder of the year. Per CEO David Steinberg's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Our first half results and strong sales pipeline provide us with the confidence and visibility to increase the midpoint of our full year revenue guidance by $21 million, which is $10 million more than our second quarter outperformance [...]

Independent agencies also continued to be a strong growth driver, adopting our platform much like an enterprise client. Since our last earnings call, we platformed three additional independent agencies. Our momentum with independent agencies is building, and the second half pipeline is robust, given that there are hundreds of additional independent agencies that we can serve.

The company also attributed its strength to its planned expansion in quota-carrying sales reps, as well as healthy consumption in the company's AI marketing cloud unit.

Zeta also excelled in profitability, despite its investments in sales. Adjusted EBITDA grew at a 53% y/y pace (nearly doubling the 28% CAGR that is called for in the company's FY28 long-term plan), with adjusted EBITDA margins hitting 19.1%, 220bps higher y/y.

Key takeaways

In my view, Zeta remains a highly underappreciated company, as evidenced by its low valuation multiples despite accelerating organic growth and substantial progress against a long-term FY28 plan that may end up proving quite conservative. Again, I'll emphasize that the possibility of inbound M&A interest is a nice cherry on top, but I think investors can still benefit from upward multiples re-rating here even if Zeta remains a standalone company. "Buy" here with confidence.

data.title: Tempus Screens Overvalued, Yet Data And Services Optionality Keeps It In Play
data.description:
Introduction

Tempus AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:TEM) remains very busy, which has inspired me to take another look at its stock. My last evaluation of Tempus was in late July. While I noted “operational progress,” Tempus’s lack of profitability and ultra-premium valuation made me cautious. I ended with a Hold rating.

As I slyly put it last time, Tempus has its “hands in multiple cookie jars.” And as I discuss in the paragraphs that follow, Tempus continues to diversify beyond core Genomics, and its financials are heading in the right direction (with some caveats). That being said, its valuation continues to demand excellent results, meriting caution.

Tempus AI Financial Performance

Before I get into its second-quarter earnings results, let’s go back to my first analysis on TEM. My discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis implied that TEM was priced for 34% Y/Y revenue growth and 15% free cash flow (FCF) margins over the nine-year projection. Given some acquisitions that have taken place since, and its change in value, I’ll revisit its valuation assumptions later on.

In Q2, Tempus generated $314.6 million (up 89.6% Y/Y). Genomics contributed $241.8 million (up 115.3% Y/Y with a gross margin of 59%). Meanwhile, Tempus’s Data and Services segment, which has a higher margin than Genomics, generated $72.8 million (up 35.7% with a gross margin of 73%). In my opinion, D&S is Tempus’s “crown jewel.” This is what gives it its premium valuation (“platform, not just a lab”). If not for D&S, Tempus would be valued more like a Genomic company. Notably, while both segments saw margin improvements, D&S growth “decelerated” (from 43.2% Y/Y). Of course, Genomic growth was largely driven by Tempus’s recent Ambry acquisition.

Although it’s not totally “apples-to-apples” (IPO effect), Tempus is showing a lot of operating leverage. Gross profit in Q2 ‘25 made up 62% of revenue. In Q2 ‘24, it was 45.5%. R&D and SG&A expenses are much lower, but some of this was IPO-driven. In Q2'25, Tempus’s operating loss was $61.8 million. Adjusted EBITDA was -$5.6 million in Q2 ‘25. As I pointed out in a July tangent, adjusted EBITDA does not come close to equaling cash generation. As is typical in its industry, stock-based compensation is significant. Q2 operating cash flow actually turned positive ($44 million) as non-cash add-backs and a $36.8 million deferred-revenue build (Pathos) more than offset accounts receivable growth. It was also aided by a step-up in gross profit. So, while positive FCF (CapEx was minimal) is a step in the right direction, it is not proof of sustainability. What to look out for in this regard? Gross margins ≥60% with OpEx intensity trending down is proof of structural leverage.

Looking ahead, management guided for $1.26 billion in FY25 revenue with an adjusted EBITDA of $5 million.

This is a perfect spot to begin modeling TEM on a cash flow basis.

TEM Stock Valuation Assumptions

TEM is “overvalued” according to many metrics. In one example, TEM’s EV/Sales (trailing twelve months) is 19.43 (a 381% difference to its peers in the Health Care sector).

My DCF model reveals that TEM is about 39% overvalued based on 30% Y/Y revenue growth and 15% FCF margin assumptions (see Appendix for all input assumptions).

Assuming FCF margin assumptions are stable at 15%, TEM appears priced for 38.8% Y/Y revenue growth.

As with any model, there are limitations with my DCF analysis. For starters, it generates a single estimate based on fixed inputs. The Monte Carlo simulation that follows allows for variation among key inputs and illustrates a distribution of possible share prices. It gives my main DCF function some "error flexibility." For instance, what if my stock beta input assumption was too high or low (±0.2)?

So, this tells me that while TEM is “overvalued” (based on assumptions of 30% Y/Y growth and 15% FCF margins), it is not “unreasonably overvalued.” I can say this because TEM’s current price ($103) falls just under the 95th percentile ($104).

Of course, 15% FCF margins do not have to be static. In reality, there is a range of growth/margin assumptions that can result in “DCF Value per Share” = “Current Stock Price.”

In one example, 41.4% Y/Y revenue growth and 13% FCF margins do the trick. Likewise, higher FCF margins allow for lower growth.

Recent Developments

As alluded to earlier, Tempus is active in many markets.

In August, Tempus acquired Paige (digital pathology) for $81.25 million. This expands its non-sequencing data moat and interference layers for the AI stack. So, this type of deal is the “outside Genomics” flywheel I’ve been hoping to see. Again, adding to D&S will lift long-term FCF margin. In addition, Tempus’s valuation relies on tangible expansion beyond testing. Likewise, in September, Tempus revealed that Northwestern Medicine became the first health system to integrate David, its generative AI co-pilot, into electronic health records. Software/device workflows, like David, have much better recurring economics, which support Tempus’s SaaS-like valuation premium. Of course, as is the case with many of Tempus's new products, whether or not they will translate into meaningful revenue remains unknown.

Final Thoughts

Given its recent performance, particularly amid a bull market, it is not particularly surprising that TEM has rallied since my last evaluation. Beyond improving financial trends (growth and adjusted EBITDA), there is clear evidence of diversification. As implied by the Q2 product mix, this is already playing out. Moreover, Paige, Pixel/ECG, xR IVD, etc., offer meaningful D&S growth opportunities.

Still, there’s reason for caution here (maintain “Hold”). As implied by my DCF analysis, TEM is priced for perfection. Reimbursement resets could trim gross margins. Moreover, the integration of Ambry/Paige and others needs clean synergy capture. Tempus is still clearly in a “growth at all costs” phase. It will be interesting to see what its business looks like (in terms of operating leverage) when it takes its foot off the M&A accelerator. However, at its current price, I’d rather either await material progress (consistent FCF generation, robust D&S revenue acceleration) or a better entry (~$60-70/share) before jumping aboard.

Appendix

DCF model assumptions:

  • Year 1 Revenue: Tempus’s FY25 guide (~$1.26 billion).

  • Fraction of Year Elapsed: 0.5 accounts for two quarters of “realized cash flow.”

  • Revenue growth: 30% is an assumption based on recent trends (before large M&A) and analyst estimates.

  • FCF Margin: 14% is an assumption based on Genomic/Data mix.

  • Beta: 1.35 (diagnostics/health-tech blend).

  • Risk-Free Rate: 10-year UST.

  • Market Risk Premium: Long-run U.S. MRP.

  • Cost of Debt: Pro forma blend: 0.75% converts dominate; revolver/term loan higher. This input does not materially impact the final result.

  • Tax Rate: Long-run normalized U.S. effective rate.

  • Terminal Growth: 2% is a long-run normalized U.S. effective rate.

  • Interest-Bearing Debt: $900 million is a pro forma estimate. Small variances (±$200 million) do not materially impact the final result.

  • Cash and Equivalents: $291 million “total cash and ST investments” as of June 30.
